section.title: The Twelve and the Sermon on the Plain
section.sectionRationale: After two clashes over the Sabbath that harden the opposition, Jesus spends a night in prayer and chooses twelve from His disciples, then comes down to a level place and preaches. Luke's Sermon on the Plain is his counterpart to Matthew's Sermon on the Mount: blessings on the poor and hungry, woes on the comfortable, the command to love enemies, warnings against a judging spirit, and a closing call to build life on obedience to His words. It is one movement that moves from conflict, to the forming of the new community, to its charter.
section.thesis: The Lord of the Sabbath forms a new people around Himself and lays out the upside-down life of His kingdom: God's blessing rests on the poor and hated who belong to Him, His people love even their enemies as sons of the merciful Father, and the one who truly hears Jesus proves it by doing what He says.
section.frameNote: Five movements: two Sabbath controversies that reveal Jesus as Lord of the Sabbath; a night of prayer and the choosing of the Twelve with healing of the crowds; the blessings and woes; the command to love enemies and refuse a judging spirit; and the tree known by its fruit with the two foundations.

units.label: The Lord of the Sabbath
units.note: grain on the Sabbath and a withered hand restored

verses.text: One Sabbath Jesus was passing through the grainfields, and His disciples began to pick the heads of grain, rub them in their hands, and eat them.
But some of the Pharisees asked, “Why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath?”
Jesus replied, “Have you not read what David did when he and his companions were hungry?
He entered the house of God, took the consecrated bread and gave it to his companions, and ate what is lawful only for the priests to eat.”
Then Jesus declared, “The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath.”
On another Sabbath Jesus entered the synagogue and was teaching, and a man was there whose right hand was withered.
Looking for a reason to accuse Jesus, the scribes and Pharisees were watching Him closely to see if He would heal on the Sabbath.
But Jesus knew their thoughts and said to the man with the withered hand, “Get up and stand among us.” So he got up and stood there.
Then Jesus said to them, “I ask you, which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it?”
And after looking around at all of them, He said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He did so, and it was restored.
But the scribes and Pharisees were filled with rage and began to discuss with one another what they might do to Jesus.

units.label: The choosing of the Twelve
units.note: a night of prayer, twelve apostles, and healing crowds

verses.text: In those days Jesus went out to the mountain to pray, and He spent the night in prayer to God.
When daylight came, He called His disciples to Him and chose twelve of them, whom He also designated as {{apostolos|apostles}}:
Simon, whom He named Peter, and his brother Andrew; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew;
Matthew and Thomas; James son of Alphaeus and Simon called the Zealot;
Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.

Then Jesus came down with them and stood on a level place. A large crowd of His disciples was there, along with a great number of people from all over Judea, Jerusalem, and the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon.
They had come to hear Him and to be healed of their diseases, and those troubled by unclean spirits were healed.
The entire crowd was trying to touch Him, because power was coming from Him and healing them all.

units.label: The blessings and the woes
units.note: God's favor on the poor, His warning to the comfortable

verses.text: Looking up at His disciples, Jesus said: “{{makarios|Blessed}} are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.
Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be filled. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh.
Blessed are you when people hate you, and when they exclude you and insult you and reject your name as evil because of the Son of Man.
Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, because great is your reward in heaven. For their fathers treated the prophets in the same way.
But woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort.
Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will hunger. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.
Woe to you when all men speak well of you, for their fathers treated the false prophets in the same way.

units.label: Love your enemies
units.note: mercy like the Father, and no judging spirit

verses.text: But to those of you who will listen, I say: {{agapao|Love}} your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.
If someone strikes you on one cheek, turn to him the other also. And if someone takes your cloak, do not withhold your tunic as well.
Give to everyone who asks you, and if anyone takes what is yours, do not demand it back.
Do to others as you would have them do to you.
If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners love those who love them.
If you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners do the same.
And if you lend to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, expecting to be repaid in full.

But love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them, expecting nothing in return. Then your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High; for He is kind to the ungrateful and wicked.
Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
Do not judge, and you will not be judged. Do not condemn, and you will not be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven.
Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over will be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured back to you.”

units.label: The tree and its fruit, and the two foundations

verses.text: Jesus also told them a parable: “Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?
A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone who is fully trained will be like his teacher.
Why do you look at the speck in your brother’s eye but fail to notice the beam in your own eye?

How can you say, ‘Brother, let me take the speck out of your eye,’ while you yourself fail to see the beam in your own eye? You hypocrite! First take the beam out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.
No good tree bears bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit.
For each tree is known by its own fruit. Indeed, figs are not gathered from thornbushes, nor grapes from brambles.

The good man brings good things out of the good treasure of his heart, and the evil man brings evil things out of the evil treasure of his heart. For out of the overflow of the heart the mouth speaks.
Why do you call Me ‘{{kyrios|Lord}}, Lord,’ but do not do what I say?
I will show you what he is like who comes to Me and hears My words and acts on them:

He is like a man building a house, who dug down deep and laid his foundation on the rock. When the flood came, the torrent crashed against that house but could not shake it, because it was well built.

But the one who hears My words and does not act on them is like a man who built his house on ground without a foundation. The torrent crashed against that house, and immediately it fell, and great was its destruction!”

apostolos.gloss: One who is sent, an envoy or commissioned messenger who carries the authority of the sender. Jesus prays all night and then names twelve, giving them this title. They are not merely learners but delegates, sent to represent Him, and the number twelve deliberately echoes the twelve tribes: Jesus is founding a renewed Israel around Himself.

makarios.gloss: Blessed, happy, and deeply well off, not because of pleasant circumstances but because of God's favor. Jesus pronounces this blessing on the poor, the hungry, the weeping, and the hated who belong to Him. Their present lack is real, but God's kingdom, fullness, laughter, and reward are certainly theirs, so they are already the truly fortunate ones.

agapao.gloss: To love with a deliberate, self-giving love that wills and does good to another, regardless of merit or return. Jesus commands this love even toward enemies, grounding it in the character of God who is kind to the ungrateful and wicked. It is not warm feeling but active goodness, the mark of those who are sons of the Most High.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, the one with rightful authority. To call Jesus 'Lord, Lord' is to acknowledge His sovereign claim, yet Jesus warns that the title is empty unless it issues in obedience. The word is also used in the Greek Old Testament for God Himself, so calling Jesus Lord carries the weight of confessing His divine authority over life.

basic.context: Two Sabbath scenes open the chapter. Jesus defends His disciples for picking grain and heals a man's withered hand, declaring Himself Lord of the Sabbath, which enrages His opponents. Then, after a whole night of prayer, He chooses twelve apostles and comes down to a level place, where crowds press to be healed. There He preaches: blessings on the poor and hungry and hated, woes on the rich and comfortable, the command to love enemies and be merciful like the Father, warnings against a judging and hypocritical spirit, and a final picture of two builders. The whole sermon asks whether we will merely call Him Lord or actually do what He says.
basic.hermeneutics: The blessings and woes are not praising poverty in itself or condemning wealth in itself; they announce a great reversal for those who belong to the Son of Man, and a warning to those whose comfort is their whole reward. The commands to turn the cheek and give the tunic are vivid pictures of a heart free from retaliation and grasping, not wooden rules that cancel all justice. The closing parable makes the test plain: real hearing shows itself in obedience.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus names twelve apostoloi, sent ones, forming a new Israel. He calls the poor makarios, truly blessed, because the kingdom is theirs. He commands His people to agapao their enemies, to love them with active goodness like the Father. And He asks why they call Him kyrios, Lord, yet do not do what He says.
basic.typology: Jesus choosing twelve apostles echoes the twelve tribes of Israel, marking Him as the founder of a renewed people of God. The Sermon on the Plain stands in the line of Moses giving the law, but now the greater Teacher speaks His own words with authority, and the house built on the rock recalls the wisdom that builds a life on the fear of the Lord.
basic.christ: Jesus speaks as Lord of the Sabbath and as the one whose words are the rock on which a life either stands or falls. He does not merely relay God's will; He is the authority. He blesses the poor and hated because they are His, and He calls people to love enemies as He Himself will love His enemies from the cross. To build on the rock is to come to Him, hear Him, and obey Him.
basic.god: God is the merciful Father who is kind even to the ungrateful and wicked, and His children are to mirror that mercy. He is the God who reverses fortunes, lifting the poor and warning the self-satisfied. And He is the one whose favor, not human approval, makes a person truly blessed.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 5 to 7 gives the fuller Sermon on the Mount. 1 Samuel 21 records David eating the consecrated bread. James 2 develops mercy and true faith that acts, and James 3 the fruit of the tongue. Leviticus 19:18 commands love of neighbor, which Jesus stretches to the enemy.
basic.oneStory: Jesus gathers a new people and hands them the charter of the kingdom. The story of Scripture has always moved toward a God who blesses the humble and lowly and asks for a love that reflects His own mercy. Here the Savior for the poor and outcast makes that explicit, and calls all who hear to build their lives on Him.

context.literary: The movement joins the end of the Galilean call narratives to Luke's great block of teaching. It is framed by conflict at the start and a call to obedience at the end. Luke's sermon is shorter than Matthew's and set on a level place rather than a mountain, with matching blessings and woes that sharpen the theme of reversal that runs through his Gospel.
context.historical: Sabbath observance was a defining mark of Jewish identity, and the Pharisees had built detailed rules around it. Jesus does not abolish the Sabbath but claims lordship over it and insists that doing good and saving life honor it. Choosing twelve apostles, and healing crowds from Judea, Jerusalem, and even the Gentile coast of Tyre and Sidon, signals a ministry that will reach beyond the narrow circle of the observant.
context.canonical: The Sermon on the Plain belongs with the Sermon on the Mount as the foundational ethics of the kingdom. Its blessings on the poor echo Isaiah's good news to the afflicted and Mary's song in Luke 1, and its call to merciful, enemy-loving love is taken up across the New Testament as the shape of Christlike life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus as Lord of the Sabbath who forms a new community around Himself, and to set out the character of that community: blessed in their poverty and need because the kingdom is theirs, marked by mercy and love of enemies, and proven genuine by obedience to His words.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Sabbath controversies describe a particular conflict and reveal Jesus' authority; the enduring point is His lordship and the priority of doing good. The blessings, woes, and commands are prescriptive for His people in every age, though the specific pictures, the cheek and the cloak, illustrate a disposition rather than issue mechanical rules.

debates.question: Are the poor blessed simply for being poor?

views.label: Literal poverty of Jesus' followers
views.case: Luke says plainly 'you who are poor' and pairs it with real hunger and weeping; his Gospel consistently shows God's special care for the materially poor and lowly, and warns the rich who trust their wealth.

views.label: The poor who belong to Christ
views.case: The blessing falls on the poor 'because of the Son of Man' and on those who are His disciples; poverty is not saving in itself, but God's kingdom belongs to the humble who have nothing to trust but Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the point is a real reversal for the lowly who are Christ's, and a sober warning to those whose comfort and reputation are their whole reward.

debates.question: Does 'do not judge' forbid all moral discernment?

views.label: A ban on a condemning, hypocritical spirit
views.case: The context is the beam in one's own eye and the measure one uses; Jesus forbids self-righteous condemnation, not honest discernment, since He immediately tells us to inspect fruit and calls some people hypocrites.

views.label: A call to leave all verdicts to God
views.case: Some stress the sweeping form, 'do not judge, and you will not be judged,' as a warning that final judgment belongs to God alone, so we must be slow to pronounce on others.

debates.bearing: Both guard against a censorious heart while leaving room for the loving discernment the same passage requires; the aim is to remove your own beam so you can help, not condemn, your brother.

study.originalLanguages: After a whole night of prayer Jesus names twelve apostoloi, sent ones, an unmistakable echo of the twelve tribes and the founding of a renewed people. Coming down to the level place, He calls the poor and hungry and hated makarios, blessed, not because their circumstances are pleasant but because God's favor and kingdom rest on them. He then commands the hardest thing, to agapao one's enemies, to will and do them good like the Father who is kind to the ungrateful. And He exposes empty religion by asking why they call Him kyrios, Lord, while refusing to do what He says, for only the one who hears and obeys builds on the rock.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus forms a renewed Israel around Himself, as the twelve tribes were the old, pointing to the church built on the apostolic foundation, Ephesians 2:20 and Revelation 21:14.

typology.type: Jesus as Lord of the Sabbath
typology.fulfillment: The rest the Sabbath foreshadowed is found in Christ Himself, who gives true rest to the weary, Matthew 11:28 and Hebrews 4:9-10.

typology.type: The house built on the rock
typology.fulfillment: The wisdom that builds a life on the fear of the Lord is fulfilled in building on Christ and His words, the only foundation that stands, 1 Corinthians 3:11.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center of the whole movement. He is Lord of the Sabbath, with authority over Israel's holiest day. He prays through the night and calls twelve to represent Him, founding a new people. He pronounces God's blessing on the poor and hated because they are His, and He commands a love of enemies that He will Himself embody when He prays for those who crucify Him. Most strikingly, He makes His own words the rock: a life stands or falls on whether it hears and obeys Him. To call Him Lord and do what He says is to build on the one foundation that the flood cannot shake.
study.god: God is revealed as the merciful Father whose children are to be merciful, kind even to the ungrateful and wicked. He is the God of the great reversal, whose kingdom belongs to the poor and whose reward awaits the persecuted, while the self-satisfied who have their comfort now are warned. And He is the God whose approval alone makes a person blessed, so that being hated for the Son of Man is cause for joy, not despair.

crossReferences.note: The parallel Beatitudes in the Sermon on the Mount, with which Luke's blessings and woes are read together.
David eating the consecrated bread, which Jesus cites to defend His disciples on the Sabbath.
Love your neighbor as yourself, which Jesus extends even to the enemy.
Be doers of the word and not hearers only, echoing the two builders who hear and either act or do not.

study.oneStory: The Savior for the poor and outcast gathers a new people around Himself and hands them the charter of His kingdom. Scripture had long promised a God who lifts the lowly and asks for mercy that mirrors His own; here Jesus makes it explicit, blesses the poor who are His, commands love even of enemies, and roots it all in coming to Him and doing what He says. The story runs on toward the cross, where this enemy-loving Lord will love His enemies to the end, and toward the house that will never fall because it is built on Him.

sermon.title: A Christian Manifesto, Part One
sermon.why: Begg preaches Luke 6:20-27, the heart of the Sermon on the Plain, showing that Jesus' blessings, woes, and command to love enemies expose our need and call for a heart transformed by Christ so that we learn to love what the world hates. A faithful, expository handling of this movement's central section.

quiz.q: What does Jesus claim about Himself in the Sabbath controversies?

options.text: That the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath, with authority over it.
options.note: Luke 6:5 records Jesus' direct claim to lordship over the Sabbath.

options.text: That doing good and saving life are always lawful on the Sabbath.
options.note: True and stated in verse 9, but it flows from His deeper claim to be Lord of the Sabbath.

options.text: That the Sabbath no longer matters at all.
options.note: Jesus does not abolish the Sabbath; He claims lordship over it and shows its true purpose.

options.text: That the Pharisees were right to accuse His disciples.
options.note: Jesus defends His disciples and exposes the accusers' hardness.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus call the poor and hungry blessed?

options.text: Because the kingdom of God is theirs, and God will fill and comfort those who are His.
options.note: The blessing rests on God's favor and kingdom, promised to the lowly who belong to the Son of Man.

options.text: Because they will one day be rich and full in this life.
options.note: There is real reversal and future fullness, but the promise is the kingdom and reward from God, not simply earthly wealth.

options.text: Because poverty is holy in itself and makes people good.
options.note: Poverty is not saving in itself; the blessing is on the humble who trust God, not on need as a virtue.

options.text: Because God ignores the poor and blesses the strong.
options.note: The opposite is true: God lifts the lowly and warns the self-satisfied.

quiz.q: What does Jesus command toward enemies?

options.text: To love them, do good to them, bless them, and pray for them, as children of the merciful Father.
options.note: Luke 6:27-36 grounds enemy-love in the character of God who is kind to the ungrateful.

options.text: To be polite to them while quietly waiting for revenge.
options.note: Outward restraint is a start, but Jesus commands active love and goodness, not a hidden desire for revenge.

options.text: To love only those who might love you back.
options.note: Jesus says even sinners love those who love them; He calls for more than that.

options.text: To repay evil with evil so justice is done.
options.note: This directly contradicts Jesus' command to love and do good to enemies.

quiz.q: According to the closing parable, what marks the person who truly hears Jesus?

options.text: He comes to Jesus, hears His words, and acts on them, building his life on the rock.
options.note: Luke 6:47-48 defines the wise builder as the one who hears and obeys.

options.text: He calls Jesus 'Lord, Lord' with sincere words.
options.note: Calling Him Lord is right, but Jesus says it is empty unless it issues in doing what He says.

options.text: He admires Jesus' teaching and finds it inspiring.
options.note: Admiration without obedience is exactly the house built without a foundation.

options.text: He hears the words but need not act on them.
options.note: That is the man whose house collapses; hearing without doing ends in ruin.

quiz.q: How does Jesus address a judging, critical spirit?

options.text: By telling us to remove the beam from our own eye before helping with the speck in another's.
options.note: Luke 6:41-42 targets self-righteous hypocrisy while still calling us to help our brother rightly.

options.text: By forbidding every kind of moral discernment about anyone.
options.note: He warns against condemnation, but the same passage tells us to inspect fruit, so it is not a ban on all discernment.

options.text: By saying our faults do not matter as long as we mean well.
options.note: Jesus takes our own faults very seriously; the beam must come out first.

options.text: By encouraging us to expose others' sins freely.
options.note: This is the opposite of His warning against a condemning spirit.

questions: Jesus prayed all night before choosing the Twelve. What would it look like for you to seek God in prayer before your next important decision?

The blessings fall on the poor, hungry, and hated who are Christ's, while the woes warn the comfortable. Where are you tempted to find your security in comfort, wealth, or the approval of others rather than in God?

Jesus commands love even of enemies, grounded in the Father's kindness to the ungrateful. Who is the hardest person for you to love, and what would one act of genuine good toward them look like this week?
Before pointing at the speck in another's eye, Jesus says to remove the beam from your own. Where might you be quicker to criticize others than to face your own faults?
Jesus asks why we call Him 'Lord, Lord' but do not do what He says. Where are you hearing His words without yet acting on them, and what would building on the rock look like there?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts read 'on the second Sabbath after the first' rather than simply 'one Sabbath'; the BSB follows the shorter, better attested reading.
The consecrated bread is literally the Bread of the Presence, set before the LORD in the tabernacle and lawful only for the priests to eat, which sharpens Jesus' point about David.
Luke says plainly 'you who are poor,' where Matthew has 'poor in spirit'; Luke's stress falls on the actual lowly and needy who belong to Jesus, a theme running through his whole Gospel.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 6:1-49. Headings, line breaks, footnote and poem markers were skipped when building verse text. Two em dashes (verses 49 in this chapter) were converted to commas. No dash characters remain.
